Vulnerability Description:
Gradio, an open-source Python package, is vulnerable to arbitrary file read due to misuse of File or UploadButton components. This could allow an attacker with access to the application to read arbitrary files from the application server.

**Note: The authoring of this BDSA has been AI-assisted. The full technical details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by the Black Duck Cybersecurity Research Center (CyRC).**

Gradio is vulnerable to access control list (ACL) bypass due to improper case normalization in file path validation. This could allow an attacker to access sensitive files by altering the letter case of blocked file or directory paths.

**Note: The authoring of this BDSA has been AI-assisted. The full technical details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by the Black Duck Cybersecurity Research Center (CyRC).**

A vulnerability in the file upload process of gradio-app/gradio version @gradio/video@0.10.2 allows for a Denial of Service (DoS) attack. An attacker can append a large number of characters to the end of a multipart boundary, causing the system to continuously process each character and issue warnings. This can render Gradio inaccessible for extended periods, disrupting services and causing significant downtime.

A Denial of Service (DoS) vulnerability was discovered in the file upload feature of gradio-app/gradio version 0.39.1. The vulnerability is due to improper handling of form-data with a large filename in the file upload request. By sending a payload with an excessively large filename, the server becomes overwhelmed and unresponsive, leading to unavailability for legitimate users.

**Note: CVE details have been utilized in generating this advisory. The details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by Black Duck CyRC.**

Gradio is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to improper handling of compressed files in the `pd.read_csv` function within the `dataframe` component. This could allow an attacker to upload a maliciously crafted zip bomb, leading to a server crash and service disruption.

A vulnerability classified as problematic has been found in gradio-app gradio up to 5.29.1. This affects the function is_valid_origin of the component cross-origin resource sharing (CORS) Handler. The manipulation of the argument localhost_aliases leads to origin validation error. It is possible to initiate the attack remotely. The complexity of an attack is rather high. The exploitability is told to be difficult. The exploit has been disclosed to the public and may be used. The vendor was contacted early about this disclosure but did not respond in any way.

**Note: CVE details have been utilized in generating this advisory. The details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by Black Duck CyRC.**

**A vulnerability has been reported in gradio with the following commentary:** 

An arbitrary file copy vulnerability in Gradio's flagging feature allows unauthenticated attackers to copy any readable file from the server's filesystem. While attackers can't read these copied files, they can cause denial-of-service (DoS) by copying large files (like /dev/urandom) to fill disk space.The flagging component doesn't properly validate file paths before copying files. Attackers can send specially crafted requests to the `/gradio_api/run/predict` endpoint to trigger these file copies.

**Source**: User-controlled `path` parameter in the flagging functionality JSON payload  
**Sink**: `shutil.copy` operation in `FileData._copy_to_dir()` method

The vulnerable code flow:
1. A JSON payload is sent to the `/gradio_api/run/predict` endpoint
2. The `path` field within `FileData` object can reference any file on the system
3. When processing this request, the `Component.flag()` method creates a `GradioDataModel` object
4. The `FileData._copy_to_dir()` method uses this path without proper validation
5. The lack of validation allows copying any file the Gradio process can read


**This vulnerability was published on the [GHSA Database](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-8jw3-6x8j-v96g) and has not been independently verified by the [Black Duck CyRC](https://www.blackduck.com/resources/cybersecurity-research-center.html) Team.**

A vulnerability in the `gradio-app/gradio` repository, version git `67e4044`, allows for path traversal on Windows OS. The implementation of the `blocked_path` functionality, which is intended to disallow users from reading certain files, is flawed. Specifically, while the application correctly blocks access to paths like `C:/tmp/secret.txt`, it fails to block access when using NTFS Alternate Data Streams (ADS) syntax, such as `C:/tmp/secret.txt::$DATA`. This flaw can lead to unauthorized reading of blocked file paths.

**Note: CVE details have been utilized in generating this advisory. The details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by Black Duck CyRC.**

Gradio is vulnerable to open redirect due to improper validation of the `_target_url` parameter in the `_redirect_to_target()` function within the OAuth flow. This could allow an attacker to redirect users to malicious external sites, potentially facilitating phishing attacks.

Gradio is vulnerable to server-side request forgery (SSRF) due to improper validation of the `proxy_url` parameter in the configuration processing of the `gr.load()` function. This could allow an attacker to make arbitrary HTTP requests from the victim's server, potentially accessing internal services, cloud metadata endpoints, or private networks.

Gradio is vulnerable to improper session handling due to the use of mocked OAuth routes in `gradio/oauth.py`. This could allow an attacker to steal the server's Hugging Face token by exploiting weak session cookie signing and decoding the session payload.

Gradio is vulnerable to absolute path traversal due to improper handling of path validation in `os.path.isabs` on Windows systems. This could allow an attacker to read arbitrary files from the server's file system without authentication.

pytest is vulnerable to remote code execution (RCE) due to a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition from the improper handling of symlinks on Linux systems. This could allow an unauthenticated local attacker to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition and potentially elevate their privileges by leveraging code execution.

pytest is vulnerable to remote code execution (RCE) due to a time-of-check/time-of-use (TOCTOU) race condition from the improper handling of symlinks on Linux systems. This could allow an unauthenticated local attacker to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition and potentially elevate their privileges by leveraging code execution.

Protobuf is vulnerable to stack overflow as it is possible to bypass the recursion depth limit in the `ParseDict` function. A remote attacker could exploit this issue by supplying the application with a maliciously crafted input in order to trigger a denial-of-service (DoS) via uncontrolled recursion.

PyTorch lacks sufficient validation of values passed to the `torch.ops.profiler._call_end_callbacks_on_jit_fut._RecordFunction` function. A passed tuple of value `None` can trigger a segmentation fault when processed by the PyTorch profiler.
PyTorch contains an improper initialization vulnerability in its Sigmoid module. An attacker could exploit this to cause unspecified impacts.

PyTorch is vulnerable to local denial-of-service (DoS) due to improper profiling finalization from a function termination process not being called before accessing profiling results. Successful exploitation of this vulnerability could result in a crash in **version 2.5**, however in **version 2.7.1** this could cause the program to hang.

PyTorch is vulnerable to memory corruption due to improper input validation in the `weights_only` unpickler. An unauthenticated remote attacker could exploit this by convincing a user to load a malicious checkpoint file (`.pth`) which could allow for arbitrary code execution. Successful exploitation could result in memory corruption, which could be leveraged to achieve more serious confidentiality, integrity, and availability impacts.

Hugging Face Transformers MobileViTV2 Deserialization of Untrusted Data Remote Code Execution Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Hugging Face Transformers. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file.

The specific flaw exists within the handling of configuration files. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of user-supplied data, which can result in deserialization of untrusted data. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current user. Was ZDI-CAN-24322.

Hugging Face Transformers MaskFormer Model Deserialization of Untrusted Data Remote Code Execution Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Hugging Face Transformers. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file.

The specific flaw exists within the parsing of model files. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of user-supplied data, which can result in deserialization of untrusted data. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current user. Was ZDI-CAN-25191.

Hugging Face Transformers Trax Model Deserialization of Untrusted Data Remote Code Execution Vulnerability. This vulnerability allows remote attackers to execute arbitrary code on affected installations of Hugging Face Transformers. User interaction is required to exploit this vulnerability in that the target must visit a malicious page or open a malicious file.

The specific flaw exists within the handling of model files. The issue results from the lack of proper validation of user-supplied data, which can result in deserialization of untrusted data. An attacker can leverage this vulnerability to execute code in the context of the current user. Was ZDI-CAN-25012.

Huggingface transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to inefficient regex handling in the `SubWordJapaneseTokenizer` class within the `tokenization_gpt_neox_japanese.py` file. This could allow an attacker to cause high CPU usage and potential application downtime by providing specially crafted input, leading to a denial-of-service (DoS) scenario.

**Note: The authoring of this BDSA has been AI-assisted. The full technical details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by the Black Duck Cybersecurity Research Center (CyRC).**

**A vulnerability has been reported in transformers with the following commentary:** 

A vulnerability in the `preprocess_string()` function of the `transformers.testing_utils` module in huggingface/transformers version v**4.48.3** allows for a Regular Expression Denial of Service (ReDoS) attack. The regular expression used to process code blocks in docstrings contains nested quantifiers, leading to exponential backtracking when processing input with a large number of newline characters. An attacker can exploit this by providing a specially crafted payload, causing high CPU usage and potential application downtime, effectively resulting in a Denial of Service (DoS) scenario.

**This vulnerability was published on the [GHSA Database](https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-qq3j-4f4f-9583) and has not been independently verified by the [Black Duck CyRC](https://www.blackduck.com/resources/cybersecurity-research-center.html) Team.**

Huggingface/transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to inefficient regex complexity in the `post_process_single()` function within the `tokenization_nougat_fast.py` file. This could allow an attacker to exploit the regex's exponential time complexity, leading to high CPU usage and potential application downtime.

**Note: The authoring of this BDSA has been AI-assisted. The full technical details of the vulnerability have not been independently verified by the Black Duck Cybersecurity Research Center (CyRC).**

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to improper input validation due to insecure URL validation in the `image_utils.py` file. This could allow an attacker to craft URLs that appear legitimate but redirect to malicious domains, potentially leading to phishing attacks, malware distribution, or data exfiltration.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to insufficient regular expression complexity. This could allow an attacker to degrade application performance and potentially cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition by providing specially crafted input strings.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to an inefficient regular expression. This could allow a remote attacker to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition by providing the application with a crafted configuration file name.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to a regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to an insecure regular expression. A remote attacker could exploit this in order to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to inefficient regex handling in the `token2json()` method of the `DonutProcessor` class. This could allow an attacker to cause excessive CPU consumption, leading to service disruption and resource exhaustion.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to inefficient regex handling in the `convert_tf_weight_name_to_pt_weight_name()` function. This could allow an attacker to cause excessive CPU consumption, leading to service disruption and resource exhaustion during model conversion processes.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to an inefficient regular expression. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability by supplying the application with a large, crafted string in order to cause cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition due to excessive resource consumption.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to inefficient regular expression usage in the `normalize_numbers` method. A remote attacker could exploit this vulnerability by supplying the application with a long, crafted string in order to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition due to excessive CPU consumption.

The `huggingface/transformers` library is vulnerable to regular expression denial-of-service (ReDoS) due to a flaw in the `AdamWeightDecay` optimizer. This vulnerability arises from the `_do_use_weight_decay` method, which processes user-controlled regular expressions in the `include_in_weight_decay` and `exclude_from_weight_decay` lists. This could allow an attacker to provide malicious regular expressions that cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to 100% CPU utilization and a denial-of-service condition.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to remote code execution (RCE) due to improper handling of malicious checkpoints in the `convert_config` functionality. This could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the affected system when a user interacts with a crafted checkpoint.

**Note:** The vendor has dismissed the vulnerability report as invalid.

Hugging Face Transformers is vulnerable to remote code execution due to deserialization of untrusted data in the Transformer-XL model. This could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code when a user visits a malicious page or opens a malicious file.

**Note:** The vendor has dismissed the vulnerability report as invalid.

This could allow an attacker to execute arbitrary code on the affected system if a user interacts with a crafted checkpoint.

urllib3 is vulnerable to data amplification due to improper handling of highly compressed data in the streaming API. A remote attacker could exploit this issue in order to create a denial-of-service (DoS) condition due to excessive resource consumption.

urllib3 is vulnerable to excessive resource consumption due to a lack of limitation on how many links are allow in the decompression chain. A remote attacker could exploit this issue by sending a HTTP response with a large amount of links in the `Content-Encoding` header in order to cause a denial-of-service (DoS) condition.

urllib3 is vulnerable to denial-of-service (DoS) due to a decompression-bomb during HTTP redirect handling through the streaming API. This could allow an unauthenticated remote attacker to trigger excessive resource consumption on the client, which could cause high CPU usage and large memory allocation. Successful exploitation could potentially lead to a crash in the application.
